payload.output:
Exit code: 0
Wall time: 0.3 seconds
Output:
The core problem
Pre-loading all 119 tool schemas into the MCP tools/list surface put ~50k tokens into every session and visibly degraded the AI after ~10–15 tool calls. The fix was to stop registering everything eagerly and instead expose a tiny always-on surface that lets the AI discover tools on demand.

The solution: lazy-loaded registry (7 always-on tools, 119 in the registry)
The server keeps a registry of 119 shared definitions (ALL_TOOL_DEFS in packages/shared/src/nas-tools.ts) but exposes only 7 small tools per session. Four of those seven are the discovery/execution machinery:

Always-on tool	Role in the lazy-load scheme
list_capabilities({ group, safety, limit })	Browse enabled operations by group or safety class without invoking anything
get_capability_details({ name })	Inspect one operation's full contract: args, safety metadata, example call, common failures, related tools
tool_search({ query, limit })	Search the registry by keyword; returns structured contracts as text
invoke_tool({ name, target, args })	Execute any registry tool by name
run_command({ target, command })	Free-form tier-1-only read shell
check_disk_space({ target })	Eager "freebie" — common enough to keep always-on
restart_nas_api({ target, confirmed })	Eager recovery tool — always available
The intended flow the AI follows: list_capabilities (browse) → get_capability_details or tool_search (inspect/find) → invoke_tool (run). FastMCP session-level instructions explicitly tell clients to browse/search/detail before most NAS tasks, then call invoke_tool with the exact returned operation name.

Why this specific design (and not the "obvious" alternative)
A key documented decision: MCP supports notifications/tools/list_changed, which would in theory let tool_search dynamically register the matched tools mid-session. They deliberately don't use it because Claude clients cache the initial tools/list and never re-fetch on the notification. So dynamic registration delivers no real context savings on actual clients. Returning the schemas as text (which Claude then uses to construct an invoke_tool call) is the only mechanism that actually saves context on real clients. The server is also fully stateless, so dynamic registration wouldn't provide durable savings across requests anyway.

Making each tool's purpose crystal-clear to the connecting AI
Several layers were built specifically so the AI understands what every tool does:

1. Rich, structured contracts returned by discovery tools. tool_search and get_capability_details don't just return a name — they return descriptions, safety class, group, parameter shapes, related tools, an example call, common failures, and the exact invoke_tool call shape to copy. This is the per-tool documentation the AI needs, delivered only when relevant rather than all upfront.

2. A keyword/group search index so the AI can find tools by intent. tool_search matches against:

A keyword index (KEYWORD_TO_GROUPS): snapshot, backup, drive, sync, disk, smart, btrfs, scrub, network, tailscale, memory, cpu, iowait, log, recover, recycle, security, volume, space, audit, etc.
Group names: system, performance, network, security, drive_sync, logs, storage, files, recovery, packages, backup, write_*, misc
Name and description substrings
Multi-word queries are AND (a tool must match all words). Exact group-name matches suppress noisier description matches. Results are scored (name match +3, description +1 per word), sorted, default limit: 8, max 30.

3. Explicit safety tiers surfaced in metadata, so the AI knows the consequence of each tool before calling:

Tier 1 — read-only, automatic
Tier 2 — reversible writes, require confirmed: true in args
Tier 3 — destructive, require confirmed: true plus an HMAC-signed token
Calling a write tool without confirmed: true returns a preview of the exact shell command (prefixed with the NAS name) instead of executing — so the AI (and operator) see exactly what would run before committing.

4. Curated grouping of all 119 tools into clear, named groups (system 11, performance 5, network 8, security 2, drive_sync 9, logs 7, storage, files 11, recovery, packages 4, backup 1, and the write_* groups), each with a one-line "what it does" description. The README catalog mirrors this for humans.

Enablement without context cost
tools-config.json (enabled_read_tools / enabled_write_tools) controls which registry tools are invokable. Tools in the registry but not enabled are compiled into the image but invisible to tool_search and rejected by invoke_tool — you can ship a tool "dark" and turn it on by editing JSON, no code deploy. This keeps the discoverable surface scoped to what's actually wanted.

How to extend it without regressing the design
Per AGENTS.md and development.md: add new capabilities to ALL_TOOL_DEFS + TOOL_GROUPS + tools-config.json, and let catalog/search/detail describe them rather than registering eagerly. New always-on tools must be added to EAGER_TOOLS in index.ts, explicitly accepting the per-session context cost — that tradeoff is called out as a deliberate quirk that should not be "fixed" by loading everything.

payload.message:
For Designflow, I’d use **the official TypeScript MCP SDK** and copy the **lazy registry pattern** from your other app.

I would **not** start with Python FastMCP. And I would be cautious about community TypeScript FastMCP unless we verify it lets us avoid eager tool registration.

The right model is:

```text
@modelcontextprotocol/sdk
  + tiny always-on MCP surface
  + lazy Designflow capability registry
  + invoke_tool dispatcher
  + Streamable HTTP for shared/remote use
  + stdio for local developer AI clients
```

Always-on tools should be something like:

```text
list_capabilities
get_capability_details
tool_search
invoke_tool
get_company_context
health/status
```

Then all real Designflow operations live in a registry, not in `tools/list`:

```text
search_rfq_items
get_rfq_item
get_item_images
get_grid_layout
search_factories
get_item_detail
get_api_docs
...
```

The big lesson from your pasted notes is absolutely the key one: **do not register every operation as an MCP tool.** That recreates the 50k-token tools/list problem. MCP clients often cache the first tools list, so dynamic re-registration is not reliable enough. Return tool contracts as text through search/detail, then execute through one `invoke_tool`.

So: yes to the **FastMCP philosophy**: clean tool definitions, rich contracts, easy discovery, tiny surface.

But for Designflow’s TypeScript/Node stack, I’d make the foundation the **official TypeScript SDK**, because it gives us direct control over transport, auth, lazy discovery, and schema exposure.

Official MCP TS SDK: [github.com/modelcontextprotocol/typescript-sdk](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/typescript-sdk)  
MCP transports: [modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/concepts/transports](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/concepts/transports)

My recommendation: build `designflow-mcp` as a Node/TypeScript service using the official SDK, with a FastMCP-inspired lazy registry. That is the safest path and lines up with the hard-earned lessons from v1.

tools.name: mcp__node_repl
tools.description:
Use `js` to run JavaScript in the persistent Node-backed kernel. When a skill or prompt says to use `node_repl`, call this server's `js` execution tool. Calls default to a 30000 ms (30 seconds) timeout when `timeout_ms` is omitted. The runtime exposes `nodeRepl.cwd`, `nodeRepl.homeDir`, `nodeRepl.tmpDir`, `nodeRepl.requestMeta`, `nodeRepl.setResponseMeta(...)`, and `await nodeRepl.emitImage(...)`. Top-level bindings persist across `js` calls until `js_reset`; do not redeclare existing `const` or `let` names. Reuse existing bindings, use top-level `var` for reusable state that may be assigned again, or choose a fresh descriptive name. Use `js_add_node_module_dir` before `js` when a skill provides an extra package directory, and use dynamic imports like `await import("playwright")` rather than filesystem paths under `./node_modules`.

Use Cases:
- Control the in-app browser in conjunction with the Browser Plugin.
- Control the Chrome browser in conjunction with the Chrome Plugin. Prefer this method of controlling Chrome over alternatives (such as Computer Use) unless the user explicitly mentions an alternative.

tools.name: js
tools.description: Run JavaScript in a persistent Node-backed kernel with top-level await. This is the JavaScript execution tool for the `node_repl` MCP server; use it whenever instructions say to use `node_repl`, the Node REPL MCP, or run Node REPL code. If `timeout_ms` is omitted, execution times out after 30000 ms (30 seconds); pass a larger `timeout_ms` for slow browser automation or other long-running operations. Use `nodeRepl.cwd`, `nodeRepl.homeDir`, and `nodeRepl.tmpDir` to inspect host paths. Use `nodeRepl.requestMeta` to inspect the current MCP request `_meta` object during a tool call. Use `nodeRepl.setResponseMeta(meta)` to attach top-level MCP result `_meta`; repeated calls shallow-merge object keys for the current tool call. Use `nodeRepl.write(text)` when you want exact text output in the tool result; it writes the string exactly as given and does not append a newline. Prefer it over `console.log(...)` for final output, JSON, or other text you plan to consume programmatically. `console.log(...)` is still useful for ad hoc debugging or object inspection because it formats values and appends line breaks automatically. Use `await nodeRepl.emitImage(imageLike)` to return images; each call adds one image to the outer tool result, so call it multiple times to emit multiple images. Supported image inputs are a data URL, inferred PNG/JPEG/WebP bytes, or `{ bytes, mimeType }`. Saved references to `nodeRepl.write(...)` and `nodeRepl.emitImage(...)` stay reusable across calls, but async callbacks that fire after a call finishes still fail because no exec is active. Top-level bindings persist across calls until `js_reset`. If a call throws, prior bindings remain available and bindings that finished initializing before the throw often remain reusable. For reusable names that may be assigned again later, prefer top-level `var name = ...`; `var` can be redeclared across calls. If you hit `SyntaxError: Identifier 'x' has already been declared`, reuse the existing binding if possible, reassign it only if it was declared with `let` or `var`, or pick a new name instead of resetting immediately; a previous `const x` cannot be changed into `var x`. Use a short `{ ... }` block only for temporary scratch names, and do not wrap an entire call in block scope if you want those names reusable later. Use dynamic imports like `await import("playwright")`, `await import("pkg")`, or `await import("./file.js")`; top-level static `import` is not supported. Import packages by package name after installing them into a directory added with `js_add_node_module_dir`, `NODE_REPL_NODE_MODULE_DIRS`, or the working directory. Do not import package entrypoints by filesystem path such as `./node_modules/playwright/index.mjs`. Imported local files must be ESM `.js` or `.mjs` files and run in the context chosen at their dynamic-import boundary, so they can also use `nodeRepl.*`, the captured `console`, and `import.meta` helpers. Bare package imports always resolve from the REPL-wide search roots (`NODE_REPL_NODE_MODULE_DIRS`, then directories later added with `js_add_node_module_dir`, then cwd), not relative to the imported file's location. Imported local files may statically import other local `.js` / `.mjs` files, available packages, and allowed Node builtins. `import.meta.resolve()` returns importable strings such as `file://...`, bare package names, and `node:...` specifiers. Local file modules reload between execs. `node:` builtins are generally available via dynamic import, but `process` / `node:process` remains blocked for now because the current Rust-server-to-Node-child transport runs over stdio and raw process streams can corrupt it. Prefer `nodeRepl.write(text)` for text output and `nodeRepl.emitImage(...)` for images.

code.description: JavaScript source to execute in the persistent Node-backed kernel. The code runs with top-level await and can use the `nodeRepl` helpers. Examples: `nodeRepl.write(nodeRepl.cwd)`, `const { chromium } = await import("playwright")`, or `await nodeRepl.emitImage(pngBuffer)`.

timeout_ms.description: Optional execution timeout in milliseconds. Defaults to 30000 (30 seconds) when omitted.

title.description: Short user-facing description of what this code block is doing. Use a few words, for example `Inspect package metadata` or `Render chart preview`.

tools.name: mcp__devops_mcp
tools.description:
This DevOps MCP intentionally exposes a small always-on MCP tool surface:
health, list_capabilities, get_capability_details, tool_search, and invoke_tool.

For every host, Docker, systemd, file, or audit-log task, call tool_search first
to find the right hidden operation. Then call invoke_tool with the exact operation
name and an args object matching the operation description. Do not assume direct
tools such as run_command, docker_ps, read_file, or service_status are listed in
tools/list; they are discoverable operations to keep client context small.

Call list_capabilities to browse by category or safety class. Call
get_capability_details for one operation's full contract, including args and
examples. Call health first when you need server context, visible tools,
registered agents, or the complete operation-name list. Treat write-capable
operations with care: this server has root-level host access, so prefer
inspection commands before state-changing commands.

tools.name: tool_search
tools.description:
Search the hidden DevOps operation registry by keyword.
Call this first for every host, Docker, file, service, or audit task. Then
call invoke_tool with the exact returned operation name and an args object.

tools.name: list_capabilities
tools.description:
Browse the hidden DevOps operation catalog without invoking anything.
Optional filters: category (docker, files, systemd, audit, host, system) and
safety (read_only, state_changing, destructive). Returns compact contracts;
call get_capability_details for full details on one operation.

import type { CapabilityGroup, ToolSafety } from "../types.js";

export const SAFETY = {
  readOnly: {
    tier: 1,
    label: "read-only",
    description: "Read-only lookup. Can run automatically.",
  } satisfies ToolSafety,
  reversibleWrite: {
    tier: 2,
    label: "reversible-write",
    description: "Reversible write. Must return a preview unless confirmed.",
  } satisfies ToolSafety,
  destructive: {
    tier: 3,
    label: "destructive",
    description: "Destructive operation. Requires confirmation and signed approval.",
  } satisfies ToolSafety,
};

export const TOOL_GROUPS: CapabilityGroup[] = [
  { name: "company_context", description: "Curated Designflow workflow and architecture context." },
  { name: "api_docs", description: "Markdown API reference documents from the Designflow repos." },
  { name: "core", description: "Core PLM/RFQ/admin/auth/file API lookups." },
  { name: "item_master", description: "Item library, item detail, and art piece lookups." },
  { name: "tracking", description: "Production, licensing, lead-time, and sample tracking lookups." },
  { name: "data_sync", description: "System-to-system Coldlion/Data Sync lookups." },
  { name: "images", description: "Image and attachment metadata, including signed/public URLs where APIs return them." },
  { name: "admin", description: "Admin reference data such as factories, tariff settings, and access metadata." },
];

export const GROUP_BY_NAME = new Map(TOOL_GROUPS.map((group) => [group.name, group]));

export const KEYWORD_TO_GROUPS: Record<string, string[]> = {
  api: ["api_docs"],
  docs: ["api_docs"],

};

+import fs from "node:fs/promises";
+import path from "node:path";
+
+export type DocKey =
+  | "api_inventory"
+  | "backend_api"
+  | "item_master_api"
+  | "tracking_api"
+  | "data_sync_api"
+  | "frontend_api"
+  | "bff_api";
+
+const DOC_PATHS: Record<DocKey, string> = {
+  api_inventory: "designflow-bff/docs/system-api-inventory.md",
+  backend_api: "designflow-backend/docs/api-reference.md",
+  item_master_api: "designflow-item-master/docs/api-reference.md",
+  tracking_api: "designflow-tracking/docs/api-reference.md",
+  data_sync_api: "designflow-data-syncing/docs/api-reference.md",
+  frontend_api: "designflow-frontend/docs/api-consumption.md",
+  bff_api: "designflow-bff/docs/api-reference.md",
+};
+
+export const readDesignflowDoc = async (cwd: string, key: DocKey, maxChars = 24000): Promise<string> => {
+  const workspaceRoot = path.resolve(cwd, "..");
+  const docPath = path.join(workspaceRoot, DOC_PATHS[key]);
+  const text = await fs.readFile(docPath, "utf8");
+
+  if (text.length <= maxChars) {
+    return text;
+  }
+
+  return `${text.slice(0, maxChars)}\n\n[Truncated at ${maxChars} characters. Re-run with a larger maxChars if needed.]`;
+};
+

*** Add File: D:\repos\dflow\designflow-mcp\README.md
+# Designflow MCP
+
+TypeScript MCP server for Designflow company context, API docs, current data, and
+image/attachment metadata.
+
+This service intentionally uses a lazy-loaded registry. It does **not** register
+every Designflow operation as an MCP tool.
+
+## Why This Shape
+
+Preloading a large tools list burns session context and can degrade AI behavior.
+This server exposes a tiny always-on MCP surface:
+
+| Tool | Purpose |
+|---|---|
+| `health` | Server status and enabled hidden capability count |
+| `list_capabilities` | Browse hidden operations by group/safety |
+| `get_capability_details` | Inspect one operation's exact contract |
+| `tool_search` | Search hidden operations by intent |
+| `invoke_tool` | Execute one hidden operation by exact name |
+
+All real Designflow operations live in `src/registry/tool-defs.ts` and are called
+through `invoke_tool`.
+
+## Transport
+
+| Mode | Command | Use |
+|---|---|---|
+| Streamable HTTP | `npm run dev:http` | Shared/remote service, future Cloud Run deployment |
+| stdio | `npm run dev:stdio` | Local AI clients that spawn MCP servers as child processes |
+
+The HTTP server exposes:
+
+```text
+GET  /health
+POST /mcp
+```
+
+It is stateless by design. It does not use dynamic `tools/list_changed`
+registration because real clients can cache the initial `tools/list`.
+
+## Setup
+
+```bash
+npm install
+cp .env.example .env
+npm run dev:http
+```
+
+For API-backed tools, set:
+
+```text
+DESIGNFLOW_USER_JWT=<user JWT>
+DESIGNFLOW_CORE_BASE_URL=http://localhost:5004/api/core
+DESIGNFLOW_ITEM_MASTER_BASE_URL=http://localhost:5004/api/item_master
+DESIGNFLOW_TRACKING_BASE_URL=http://localhost:5004/api/tracking
+DESIGNFLOW_DATA_SYNC_BASE_URL=http://localhost:5004/api/data_sync
+DFLOW_SYNC_API_KEY=<optional data-sync key>
+```
+
+Without a JWT, documentation tools still work, but Designflow API calls will fail
+with auth errors.
+
+## Tool Flow
+
+Recommended client flow:
+
+```text
+tool_search({ query: "rfq price code" })
+get_capability_details({ name: "find_rfq_item_by_price_code" })
+invoke_tool({ name: "find_rfq_item_by_price_code", args: { priceCode: "ABC123" } })
+```
+
+## Extending
+
+To add an operation:
+
+1. Add a `ToolDef` to `src/registry/tool-defs.ts`.
+2. Add its name to `tools-config.json`.
+3. Give it a clear group, safety tier, args description, example args, common
+   failures, and related tools.
+4. Do not register it directly in `src/server.ts`.
+
+New always-on tools are expensive because they permanently increase every
+session's MCP tools surface. Add one only after explicitly accepting that context
+cost.
+
+## Current Contract Status
+
+This first version is read-only. Write/destructive capabilities are deliberately
+not enabled.
+
*** Add File: D:\repos\dflow\designflow-mcp\AGENTS.md
+# AGENTS.md - Designflow MCP
+
+This repo implements Designflow's MCP server in TypeScript.
+
+## Prime Directive
+
+Do not eagerly register Designflow data operations as MCP tools.
+
+The MCP-visible surface should stay tiny:
+
+- `health`
+- `list_capabilities`
+- `get_capability_details`
+- `tool_search`
+- `invoke_tool`
+
+All real operations belong in `src/registry/tool-defs.ts` and are invoked through
+`invoke_tool`.
+
+## Why
+
+Large eager `tools/list` payloads waste session context and degrade AI behavior.
+MCP `tools/list_changed` is not enough to solve this because clients may cache
+the first tool list. Returning hidden operation contracts as text through
+discovery tools is the strategy that actually keeps context small.
+
+## Extension Rules
+
+When adding a hidden capability:
+
+1. Add a typed `ToolDef` in `src/registry/tool-defs.ts`.
+2. Add the tool name to `tools-config.json` if it should be enabled.
+3. Include:
+   - clear description
+   - group
+   - safety tier
+   - argument schema
+   - argument shape text
+   - example `invoke_tool` args
+   - common failures
+   - related tools
+4. Keep the operation read-only unless the write path has preview/confirmation
+   and audit behavior.
+
+## Safety
+
+This version enables read-only capabilities only. Write tools must not be added
+without a preview-first flow and explicit confirmation contract.
+
+## Runtime
+
+- Node.js 20+
+- official `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk`
+- Streamable HTTP for remote/shared clients
+- stdio for local child-process clients
+

+

+
+Designflow MCP is a thin TypeScript service that exposes Designflow context and

+It does not duplicate backend business logic. API-backed tools call existing

+
+## Always-On Tools
+
+The always-on MCP tools are intentionally small:
+
+| Tool | Description |
+|---|---|
+| `health` | Server status |
+| `list_capabilities` | Browse enabled hidden operations |
+| `get_capability_details` | Inspect one operation contract |
+| `tool_search` | Search hidden operations by intent |

+
+The service supports:

+- stateless Streamable HTTP: `POST /mcp`
+- stdio: child-process mode for local AI clients

D:\\repos\\dflow\\designflow-mcp\\docs\\.content:
# Designflow MCP Architecture

## Overview

Designflow MCP is a thin TypeScript service that exposes Designflow context and
read-only lookups to AI clients.

It does not duplicate backend business logic. API-backed tools call existing
Designflow services through the BFF-compatible base URLs.

```text
AI client
  -> designflow-mcp
  -> lazy registry discovery
  -> invoke_tool
  -> Designflow BFF/backend APIs
```

## Always-On Tools

The always-on MCP tools are intentionally small:

| Tool | Description |
|---|---|
| `health` | Server status |
| `list_capabilities` | Browse enabled hidden operations |
| `get_capability_details` | Inspect one operation contract |
| `tool_search` | Search hidden operations by intent |
| `invoke_tool` | Execute a hidden operation |

## Hidden Registry

Hidden operations are defined in:

```text
src/registry/tool-defs.ts
```

Enablement is controlled by:

```text
tools-config.json
DESIGNFLOW_MCP_ENABLED_TOOLS
DESIGNFLOW_MCP_DISABLED_TOOLS
```

Compiled but disabled tools are not discoverable and cannot be invoked.

## Transports

The service supports:

- stateless Streamable HTTP: `POST /mcp`
- stdio: child-process mode for local AI clients

Stateless HTTP is used first to keep Cloud Run deployment simple. If future MCP
features need server-initiated notifications or resumability, add a stateful
transport map carefully and preserve the lazy tools-list strategy.

## Auth

Read-only API tools use environment-provided credentials:

- `DESIGNFLOW_USER_JWT` for user-scoped Designflow API calls
- `DFLOW_SYNC_API_KEY` for data sync calls

Future production deployment should replace static env JWT usage with a proper
user-scoped or service-scoped auth flow.

# Designflow MCP

TypeScript MCP server for Designflow company context, API docs, current data, and
image/attachment metadata.

This service intentionally uses a lazy-loaded registry. It does **not** register
every Designflow operation as an MCP tool.

## Why This Shape

Preloading a large tools list burns session context and can degrade AI behavior.
This server exposes a tiny always-on MCP surface:

| Tool | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `health` | Server status and enabled hidden capability count |
| `list_capabilities` | Browse hidden operations by group/safety |
| `get_capability_details` | Inspect one operation's exact contract |
| `tool_search` | Search hidden operations by intent |
| `invoke_tool` | Execute one hidden operation by exact name |

All real Designflow operations live in `src/registry/tool-defs.ts` and are called
through `invoke_tool`.

## Transport

| Mode | Command | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Streamable HTTP | `npm run dev:http` | Shared/remote service, future Cloud Run deployment |
| stdio | `npm run dev:stdio` | Local AI clients that spawn MCP servers as child processes |

The HTTP server exposes:

```text
GET  /health
POST /mcp
```

It is stateless by design. It does not use dynamic `tools/list_changed`
registration because real clients can cache the initial `tools/list`.

## Setup

```bash
npm install
cp .env.example .env
npm run dev:http
```

For API-backed tools, set:

```text
DESIGNFLOW_USER_JWT=<user JWT>
DESIGNFLOW_CORE_BASE_URL=http://localhost:5004/api/core
DESIGNFLOW_ITEM_MASTER_BASE_URL=http://localhost:5004/api/item_master
DESIGNFLOW_TRACKING_BASE_URL=http://localhost:5004/api/tracking
DESIGNFLOW_DATA_SYNC_BASE_URL=http://localhost:5004/api/data_sync
DFLOW_SYNC_API_KEY=<optional data-sync key>
```

Without a JWT, documentation tools still work, but Designflow API calls will fail
with auth errors.

## Tool Flow

Recommended client flow:

```text
tool_search({ query: "rfq price code" })
get_capability_details({ name: "find_rfq_item_by_price_code" })
invoke_tool({ name: "find_rfq_item_by_price_code", args: { priceCode: "ABC123" } })
```

## Extending

To add an operation:

1. Add a `ToolDef` to `src/registry/tool-defs.ts`.
2. Add its name to `tools-config.json`.
3. Give it a clear group, safety tier, args description, example args, common
   failures, and related tools.
4. Do not register it directly in `src/server.ts`.

New always-on tools are expensive because they permanently increase every
session's MCP tools surface. Add one only after explicitly accepting that context
cost.

## Current Contract Status

This first version is read-only. Write/destructive capabilities are deliberately
not enabled.

# AGENTS.md - Designflow MCP

This repo implements Designflow's MCP server in TypeScript.

## Prime Directive

Do not eagerly register Designflow data operations as MCP tools.

The MCP-visible surface should stay tiny:

- `health`
- `list_capabilities`
- `get_capability_details`
- `tool_search`
- `invoke_tool`

All real operations belong in `src/registry/tool-defs.ts` and are invoked through
`invoke_tool`.

## Why

Large eager `tools/list` payloads waste session context and degrade AI behavior.
MCP `tools/list_changed` is not enough to solve this because clients may cache
the first tool list. Returning hidden operation contracts as text through
discovery tools is the strategy that actually keeps context small.

## Extension Rules

When adding a hidden capability:

1. Add a typed `ToolDef` in `src/registry/tool-defs.ts`.
2. Add the tool name to `tools-config.json` if it should be enabled.
3. Include:
   - clear description
   - group
   - safety tier
   - argument schema
   - argument shape text
   - example `invoke_tool` args
   - common failures
   - related tools
4. Keep the operation read-only unless the write path has preview/confirmation
   and audit behavior.

## Safety

This version enables read-only capabilities only. Write tools must not be added
without a preview-first flow and explicit confirmation contract.

## Runtime

- Node.js 20+
- official `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk`
- Streamable HTTP for remote/shared clients
- stdio for local child-process clients

+FROM node:22-slim AS build
+
+WORKDIR /app
+COPY package*.json ./
+RUN npm ci
+COPY tsconfig.json ./
+COPY src ./src
+RUN npm run build
+
+FROM node:22-slim AS runtime
+
+ENV NODE_ENV=production
+WORKDIR /app
+COPY package*.json ./
+RUN npm ci --omit=dev
+COPY --from=build /app/dist ./dist
+COPY tools-config.json ./tools-config.json
+COPY README.md ./README.md
+
+EXPOSE 3010
+CMD ["node", "dist/index.js", "--http"]
+
*** Add File: D:\repos\dflow\designflow-mcp\.dockerignore
+node_modules
+dist
+.env
+.env.*
+npm-debug.log
+
*** End Patch

FROM node:22-slim AS build

WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci
COPY tsconfig.json ./
COPY src ./src

FROM node:22-slim AS runtime

ENV NODE_ENV=production

COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci --omit=dev
COPY --from=build /app/dist ./dist
COPY tools-config.json ./tools-config.json
COPY README.md ./README.md

EXPOSE 3010
CMD ["node", "dist/index.js", "--http"]

payload.message:
Built it: [designflow-mcp](D:/repos/dflow/designflow-mcp/README.md)

What’s in place:
- TypeScript/Node only. No Python.
- Official `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk`, using the stable v1 package path.
- Stateless Streamable HTTP server at `POST /mcp`.
- stdio mode for local AI clients.
- Lazy registry pattern copied from the lesson learned:
  - visible MCP tools: `health`, `list_capabilities`, `get_capability_details`, `tool_search`, `invoke_tool`
  - hidden Designflow operations live in [tool-defs.ts](D:/repos/dflow/designflow-mcp/src/registry/tool-defs.ts)
  - enablement lives in [tools-config.json](D:/repos/dflow/designflow-mcp/tools-config.json)
- Read-only Designflow API/doc capabilities for RFQ, item images, item master, tracking/sample photos, factories, and API docs.
- Dockerfile for future Cloud Run deployment.
- Guardrail docs in [AGENTS.md](D:/repos/dflow/designflow-mcp/AGENTS.md) so future work doesn’t accidentally eager-register everything.

Verified:
- `npm run build` passes.
- stdio MCP smoke test shows exactly five visible tools.
- `tool_search` returns hidden tool contracts as text.
- HTTP `/health` returns `{"status":"ok","service":"designflow-mcp"}`.

I used the official TS SDK docs. Important detail: the SDK repo says `main` is v2 pre-alpha and v1.x is still recommended for production right now, so this scaffold uses `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk` v1 style rather than v2 packages. Sources: [TypeScript SDK](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/typescript-sdk), [MCP transports](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/concepts/transports).

This is a new local sibling service, not yet a GitHub repo. The next clean move is creating `popcre/designflow-mcp`, adding the remote, and pushing this scaffold to `sandbox-albert`.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

Use the `request_user_input` tool only when it is listed in the available tools for this turn.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

content.text:
1.	Before adding new code to my branch sandbox-albert, make sure you pull the latest changes from develop on github.com into sandbox-albert on github.com, then pull github.com’s sandbox-albert into this local sandbox-albert, and then start adding your code.
2.	read all the .md files. do all coding in accordance with search_docs in the AG-Grid MCP server (Angular 35.1.0 is the latest version it exposes, use that for AG Grid docs lookups), and then remove from your context window anything that doesn't help you solve the below problem
3.	We will only be working on my branch (sandbox-albert), never touching any other branch aside from develop, and never merging to main
4.	ensure that you add unit test cases for the functions/code you create, to make sure future changes do not break the functionality.

payload.message:
1.	Before adding new code to my branch sandbox-albert, make sure you pull the latest changes from develop on github.com into sandbox-albert on github.com, then pull github.com’s sandbox-albert into this local sandbox-albert, and then start adding your code.
2.	read all the .md files. do all coding in accordance with search_docs in the AG-Grid MCP server (Angular 35.1.0 is the latest version it exposes, use that for AG Grid docs lookups), and then remove from your context window anything that doesn't help you solve the below problem
3.	We will only be working on my branch (sandbox-albert), never touching any other branch aside from develop, and never merging to main
4.	ensure that you add unit test cases for the functions/code you create, to make sure future changes do not break the functionality.

Got it. I’ll follow this workflow from here on:

1. Sync `develop` from GitHub into GitHub/local `sandbox-albert` before adding code.

Exit code: 0
Wall time: 0.3 seconds
Output:
AI TASK SPEC: Repository Documentation Maintenance
ROLE
You are an AI coding agent updating Markdown documentation for a software repository.
Your documentation must help future developers and future AI coding sessions understand the actual current state of the repository without relying on prior chat context.
Documentation is part of the work. It is not optional and not a final cleanup step.
________________________________________
PRIMARY OBJECTIVE
Make AGENTS.md the canonical operating guide and documentation router for future developers and AI coding sessions.
AGENTS.md MUST let a new senior engineer or AI session understand the repository in under 5 minutes.
AGENTS.md MUST prevent future AI sessions from ingesting every .md file unnecessarily.
________________________________________
ABSOLUTE RULES
•	MUST derive documentation from the actual repository state.
•	MUST inspect relevant code, config, scripts, workflows, migrations, deployment files, and existing docs before editing docs.
•	MUST NOT invent architecture, intent, future plans, deployment behavior, credentials, identifiers, business rules, or undocumented conventions.
•	MUST NOT document guesses as facts.
•	MUST mark unknowns clearly.
•	MUST explain how unknowns can be verified.
•	MUST NOT include secrets, tokens, passwords, private keys, or production credential values.
•	MUST keep documentation high-signal and specific.
•	MUST remove or fix stale, misleading, redundant, or vague documentation.
•	MUST avoid duplicating the same guidance across multiple docs.
•	MUST update docs in the same commit as code when the code change affects docs.
•	MUST explicitly say when a change does not require documentation updates.
________________________________________
REQUIRED DOCUMENTATION FILE ROLES
Use these roles exactly:
File	Role
README.md	Quick entry point and orientation
AGENTS.md	Canonical AI/developer operating guide
CLAUDE.md	Claude Code-specific instructions only
docs/architecture.md	System design, components, data flow, constraints
docs/development.md	Local setup, run/test/lint/debug workflow
docs/configuration.md	Environment variables, config files, feature flags
docs/deployment.md	Deploy/release/environment/rollback workflow
folder-level README.md	Local context for that folder only, when genuinely useful
HANDOFF.md	Temporary continuation document for unfinished work
Create missing docs only when useful.
Delete, rewrite, or consolidate docs that are obsolete, misleading, duplicated, or too vague to help future sessions.
________________________________________
REQUIRED AGENTS.md CONTENT
AGENTS.md MUST contain the sections below.
Use actual repository paths, commands, identifiers, and deployment details.
Do not leave generic placeholders unless the value is truly unknown.
If unknown, write unknown and explain how to verify it.
________________________________________
1. Project summary
MUST include one short business/technical summary.
The summary MUST explain:
•	what the project does
•	who uses it
•	key moving parts
•	the outcome that matters
Keep this brief.
________________________________________
2. Multi-model AI note
MUST include this text near the top:
## Multi-model AI note

There is no universal ignore-file standard across AI coding tools.

`.claudeignore` works for Claude Code.

When using any other AI tool, paste this file as your first message and follow the instructions in the "What to ignore" section.
________________________________________
3. Documentation map: what to read for each task
MUST include this section near the top.
Purpose: prevent future AI sessions from loading every .md file.
Use this structure:
## Documentation map: what to read for each task

Always start with:

- `AGENTS.md`

Then load additional docs only when relevant:

| Task / question | Read these docs | Usually do not need |
|---|---|---|
| Quick repo orientation | `README.md`, `AGENTS.md` | Deep docs under `docs/` unless task requires them |
| Modify app behavior or project-owned code | `AGENTS.md`, relevant folder-level `README.md`, `docs/architecture.md` if system design is affected | `docs/deployment.md` unless deploy behavior changes |
| Add or change configuration, env vars, feature flags, secrets, or runtime settings | `AGENTS.md`, `docs/configuration.md`, `docs/deployment.md` if prod/runtime env is affected | Unrelated architecture docs |
| Change local setup, dev scripts, test/lint/debug workflow, package scripts, or tooling | `AGENTS.md`, `docs/development.md`, relevant package/config files | `docs/deployment.md` unless CI/CD changes |
| Change deployment, Docker, CI/CD, hosting, release flow, rollback, or runtime environment | `AGENTS.md`, `docs/deployment.md`, `docs/configuration.md`, relevant workflow/deployment files | Local-only development docs unless needed |
| Change database schema, migrations, models, external IDs, or data flow | `AGENTS.md`, `docs/architecture.md`, `docs/configuration.md` if env/config is affected, relevant migration/model docs | Deployment docs unless rollout/deploy behavior changes |
| Investigate bugs or incidents | `AGENTS.md`, relevant docs based on affected area, `HANDOFF.md` if present, Critical incidents section in `AGENTS.md` | Unrelated folder-level READMEs |
| Continue unfinished work | `AGENTS.md`, `HANDOFF.md`, relevant docs named inside `HANDOFF.md` | Docs unrelated to the handoff scope |
| Work in a subfolder with its own README | `AGENTS.md`, that folder-level `README.md`, and only broader docs referenced there | Other folder-level READMEs |
| Claude Code session | `CLAUDE.md`, then `AGENTS.md` | Other docs unless task requires them |
| Documentation-only cleanup | `AGENTS.md`, `README.md`, affected docs under `docs/`, folder-level READMEs only where relevant | Source files except as needed to verify accuracy |
Rules:
•	MUST be task-based.
•	MUST NOT become a flat list of every Markdown file.
•	MUST distinguish always-read docs from task-relevant docs.
•	MUST identify docs that are usually unnecessary.
•	MUST mention HANDOFF.md as required reading when it exists.
•	MUST be updated when documentation files are added, removed, renamed, or repurposed.
•	MUST reduce context bloat.
________________________________________
4. Repository structure
MUST describe important directories.
MUST separate:
•	project-owned code
•	generated code
•	third-party/vendor/framework code
•	build artifacts
•	docs
•	scripts
•	migrations
•	deployment files
Use real paths.
________________________________________
5. Prime Directive: custom-code boundary
MUST explicitly list where project-owned custom code belongs.
Use this format and adapt paths to the repo:
## Prime Directive: custom-code boundary

Our custom code lives here:

- `src/modules/[project-name]/`
- `src/app/[project-owned-routes]/`
- `docs/`
- `.github/workflows/`

Everything else requires justification before touching.
Purpose: prevent AI agents from scattering project logic into unrelated framework, vendor, generated, or third-party files.
________________________________________
6. Core modification inventory
MUST list any modified files outside project-owned areas.
MUST keep the section even if empty.
Use this format:
## Core modification inventory

| File | Change made | Why it was necessary | Risk during upgrades |
|---|---|---|---|
| `path/to/file` | Short description | Specific reason | What to check later |
If no files outside project-owned areas were modified, write that explicitly.
________________________________________
7. Task-to-file navigation: what to edit for common changes
This is NOT the same as the documentation map.
Documentation map = what docs to read.
Task-to-file navigation = what source/config/deployment files to edit or avoid.
Use this format:
## Task-to-file navigation: what to edit for common changes

| Task | Files to touch | Files not to touch |
|---|---|---|
| Change login screen | `src/...` | `vendor/...` |
| Add database field | `db/migrations/...`, `src/models/...` | Existing applied migrations |
| Add new setting | `src/config/...`, `docs/configuration.md` | Production `.env` directly |
MUST use actual repo paths when known.
________________________________________
8. Data model and external identifiers
MUST document important:
•	entities
•	fields
•	UUIDs
•	container names
•	project IDs
•	webhook names
•	deployment targets
•	external-system IDs
Use this format:
## Data model and external identifiers

| Entity/System | Identifier | Where defined | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Customer | `customer_id` | database | UUID |
| Coolify app | `[uuid]` | Coolify | Production deploy target |
| Supabase project | `[project-id]` | Supabase | Database backend |
MUST NOT casually rename, regenerate, or replace documented identifiers.
________________________________________
9. Container and service inventory
MUST list every relevant runtime service/container.
Use this format:
## Container and service inventory

| Container/service | Purpose | Managed by | App/project ID | Image/source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `app-web` | Frontend web app | Coolify | `[uuid]` | `ghcr.io/...` |
| `app-db` | Database | Compose/Coolify | `[uuid]` | postgres |
If there are no containers/services, say so.
________________________________________
10. What to ignore
MUST list files/directories that exist but should not consume AI context.
Include relevant examples such as:
•	node_modules/
•	dist/
•	.cache/
•	coverage/
•	generated SDKs
•	vendor examples
•	third-party docs
•	build artifacts
MUST match ignore-file entries where appropriate.
________________________________________
11. Intentional quirks and non-obvious decisions
MUST document anything that looks wrong, duplicated, overcomplicated, unusual, or counterintuitive but is intentional.
Use this exact format:
### [Decision name]

Looks like:
[How a new developer or AI session might misread it.]

Actually:
[What it really is.]

Why:
[Constraint, incident, requirement, or tradeoff.]

Do not change because:
[What would break or become worse.]
MUST NOT invent quirks.
________________________________________
12. Credentials and environment
MUST list required variables without secret values.
Use this format:
## Credentials and environment

| Variable | Purpose | Stored where | Required in dev | Required in prod |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `DATABASE_URL` | Database connection | Coolify env | yes | yes |
| `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` | AI model calls | Coolify env | yes | yes |
MUST NOT include actual secret values.
MUST include variables found in:
•	example env files
•	code
•	config files
•	deployment files
•	workflows
•	docs
________________________________________
13. Deployment
MUST document the real deployment path.
Include:
•	GitHub Actions workflow name
•	image/package names
•	tag pattern
•	deployment platform/app/project ID
•	deploy trigger method
•	rollback method
•	where runtime environment variables live
•	whether SSH is allowed
•	whether SSH is routine or exceptional
MUST NOT describe an ideal deployment process unless it is the real process.
________________________________________
14. Critical incidents
MUST document disasters, near-misses, migrations, broken deploys, data loss risks, or important recovery lessons.
MUST keep the section even if empty.
Use this format:
## Critical incidents

### [YYYY-MM-DD] [Incident title]

What happened:
...

Impact:
...

Root cause:
...

Recovery:
...

Rule added to prevent recurrence:
...
If there are no known incidents, write that directly.
________________________________________
15. Pending work
MUST keep pending work current.
Use this format:
## Pending work

| Status | Item | Owner/next action |
|---|---|---|
| open | Add email sending | Needs SMTP credentials |
| blocked | Deploy production | Waiting for Coolify app ID |
| done | Initial Dockerfile | Completed in commit `[sha]` |
MUST remove completed stale items or mark them done.
MUST NOT leave stale pending work.
________________________________________
CLAUDE.md RULES
CLAUDE.md MUST stay short.
CLAUDE.md MUST NOT duplicate AGENTS.md.
CLAUDE.md MAY include only:
•	Read AGENTS.md first
•	Claude Code-specific memory/context notes
•	.claudeignore guidance
•	allowed operations/tools/APIs
•	SSH permissions, if any
•	explicit note that SSH is not the normal deployment path unless actually true
•	commit style preferences
•	Claude-specific behaviors to enable or suppress
If guidance applies to all AI agents or all developers, put it in AGENTS.md, not CLAUDE.md.
________________________________________
IGNORE-FILE RULES
MUST create or update these files when appropriate:
•	.claudeignore
•	.cursorignore
MUST use matching content unless there is a tool-specific reason not to.
Standard entries:
dist/
node_modules/
.cache/
coverage/
MUST add generated, third-party, vendor, build, or cache directories that should not consume AI context.
MUST create .copilotignore if the repository uses GitHub Copilot and ignore rules matter there.
Ignore files MUST align with the “What to ignore” section of AGENTS.md.
________________________________________
HANDOFF RULE
IF any work is unfinished, blocked, or in progress:
•	MUST create HANDOFF.md in repo root.
•	MUST make it comprehensive enough for a new developer or AI session to continue without prior chat context.
•	MUST mention HANDOFF.md in AGENTS.md as required reading for continuation work.
HANDOFF.md MUST include:
•	what was being built or fixed and why
•	what is fully done
•	what is partially done and exact current state
•	future plans discussed during the session
•	what has not been started
•	decisions made and why
•	dead ends or abandoned approaches
•	exact next action
•	known risks, blockers, or unknowns
•	session context that would otherwise be lost
IF the work described in HANDOFF.md is complete:
•	MUST delete HANDOFF.md.
________________________________________
REQUIRED AI WORKFLOW
Follow this sequence:
1.	Inspect repository structure.
2.	Identify owned code, generated code, vendor/framework code, docs, scripts, migrations, deployment files, and build artifacts.
3.	Inspect existing Markdown docs.
4.	Determine which docs are stale, missing, redundant, or misleading.
5.	Update AGENTS.md first.
6.	Update supporting docs only where topic-specific detail belongs.
7.	Update README.md only for quick orientation.
8.	Update CLAUDE.md only for Claude-specific notes.
9.	Update folder-level README.md files only where local folder context is useful.
10.	Update .claudeignore, .cursorignore, and .copilotignore if applicable.
11.	Create, update, or delete HANDOFF.md according to the handoff rule.
12.	Verify documentation against actual repository files.
13.	Check that no secrets were added.
14.	Commit and push documentation changes to GitHub.
15.	Provide final completion report.
________________________________________
VERIFICATION GATES
Before final response, verify:
•	AGENTS.md exists.
•	AGENTS.md contains the required sections.
•	AGENTS.md includes the documentation map.
•	Documentation map routes tasks to docs instead of telling agents to read everything.
•	CLAUDE.md does not duplicate AGENTS.md.
•	Ignore files match documented ignore guidance where appropriate.
•	Env vars are listed without secret values.
•	Deployment docs describe actual deployment, not guesses.
•	HANDOFF.md exists if and only if work is unfinished, blocked, or in progress.
•	No secrets were written.
•	Stale docs were removed, corrected, or clearly marked.
________________________________________
FINAL COMPLETION REPORT
After committing and pushing, final response MUST include:
## Documentation changes

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `path/to/file.md` | Summary of change |

## Ignore files

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `.claudeignore` | Summary of change |

## Handoff status

- `HANDOFF.md`: present/absent
- Reason: ...

## Verification

- Docs verified against actual repo: yes/no
- Secrets added: yes/no
- Unknowns remaining: ...

## Remaining documentation gaps

- ...
If no documentation changes were required, final response MUST say so and explain why.

# Designflow MCP

TypeScript MCP server for Designflow company context, API docs, current data, and
image/attachment metadata.

This service intentionally uses a lazy-loaded registry. It does **not** register
every Designflow operation as an MCP tool.

## Why This Shape

Preloading a large tools list burns session context and can degrade AI behavior.
This server exposes a tiny always-on MCP surface:

| Tool | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `health` | Server status and enabled hidden capability count |
| `list_capabilities` | Browse hidden operations by group/safety |
| `get_capability_details` | Inspect one operation's exact contract |
| `tool_search` | Search hidden operations by intent |
| `invoke_tool` | Execute one hidden operation by exact name |

All real Designflow operations live in `src/registry/tool-defs.ts` and are called
through `invoke_tool`.

## Transport

| Mode | Command | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Streamable HTTP | `npm run dev:http` | Shared/remote service, future Cloud Run deployment |
| stdio | `npm run dev:stdio` | Local AI clients that spawn MCP servers as child processes |

The HTTP server exposes:

```text
GET  /health
POST /mcp
```

It is stateless by design. It does not use dynamic `tools/list_changed`
registration because real clients can cache the initial `tools/list`.

## Setup

```bash
npm install
cp .env.example .env
npm run dev:http
```

For API-backed tools, set:

```text
DESIGNFLOW_USER_JWT=<user JWT>
DESIGNFLOW_CORE_BASE_URL=http://localhost:5004/api/core
DESIGNFLOW_ITEM_MASTER_BASE_URL=http://localhost:5004/api/item_master
DESIGNFLOW_TRACKING_BASE_URL=http://localhost:5004/api/tracking
DESIGNFLOW_DATA_SYNC_BASE_URL=http://localhost:5004/api/data_sync
DFLOW_SYNC_API_KEY=<optional data-sync key>
```

Without a JWT, documentation tools still work, but Designflow API calls will fail
with auth errors.

## Tool Flow

Recommended client flow:

```text
tool_search({ query: "rfq price code" })
get_capability_details({ name: "find_rfq_item_by_price_code" })
invoke_tool({ name: "find_rfq_item_by_price_code", args: { priceCode: "ABC123" } })
```

## Extending

To add an operation:

1. Add a `ToolDef` to `src/registry/tool-defs.ts`.
2. Add its name to `tools-config.json`.
3. Give it a clear group, safety tier, args description, example args, common
   failures, and related tools.
4. Do not register it directly in `src/server.ts`.

New always-on tools are expensive because they permanently increase every
session's MCP tools surface. Add one only after explicitly accepting that context
cost.

## Current Contract Status

This first version is read-only. Write/destructive capabilities are deliberately
not enabled.

# AGENTS.md - Designflow MCP

This repo implements Designflow's MCP server in TypeScript.

## Prime Directive

Do not eagerly register Designflow data operations as MCP tools.

The MCP-visible surface should stay tiny:

- `health`
- `list_capabilities`
- `get_capability_details`
- `tool_search`
- `invoke_tool`

All real operations belong in `src/registry/tool-defs.ts` and are invoked through
`invoke_tool`.

## Why

Large eager `tools/list` payloads waste session context and degrade AI behavior.
MCP `tools/list_changed` is not enough to solve this because clients may cache
the first tool list. Returning hidden operation contracts as text through
discovery tools is the strategy that actually keeps context small.

## Extension Rules

When adding a hidden capability:

1. Add a typed `ToolDef` in `src/registry/tool-defs.ts`.
2. Add the tool name to `tools-config.json` if it should be enabled.
3. Include:
   - clear description
   - group
   - safety tier
   - argument schema
   - argument shape text
   - example `invoke_tool` args
   - common failures
   - related tools
4. Keep the operation read-only unless the write path has preview/confirmation
   and audit behavior.

## Safety

This version enables read-only capabilities only. Write tools must not be added
without a preview-first flow and explicit confirmation contract.

## Runtime

- Node.js 20+
- official `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk`
- Streamable HTTP for remote/shared clients
- stdio for local child-process clients

# Designflow MCP Architecture

## Overview

Designflow MCP is a thin TypeScript service that exposes Designflow context and
read-only lookups to AI clients.

It does not duplicate backend business logic. API-backed tools call existing
Designflow services through the BFF-compatible base URLs.

```text
AI client
  -> designflow-mcp
  -> lazy registry discovery
  -> invoke_tool
  -> Designflow BFF/backend APIs
```

## Always-On Tools

The always-on MCP tools are intentionally small:

| Tool | Description |
|---|---|
| `health` | Server status |
| `list_capabilities` | Browse enabled hidden operations |
| `get_capability_details` | Inspect one operation contract |
| `tool_search` | Search hidden operations by intent |
| `invoke_tool` | Execute a hidden operation |

## Hidden Registry

Hidden operations are defined in:

```text
src/registry/tool-defs.ts
```

Enablement is controlled by:

```text
tools-config.json
DESIGNFLOW_MCP_ENABLED_TOOLS
DESIGNFLOW_MCP_DISABLED_TOOLS
```

Compiled but disabled tools are not discoverable and cannot be invoked.

## Transports

The service supports:

- stateless Streamable HTTP: `POST /mcp`
- stdio: child-process mode for local AI clients

Stateless HTTP is used first to keep Cloud Run deployment simple. If future MCP
features need server-initiated notifications or resumability, add a stateful
transport map carefully and preserve the lazy tools-list strategy.

## Auth

Read-only API tools use environment-provided credentials:

- `DESIGNFLOW_USER_JWT` for user-scoped Designflow API calls
- `DFLOW_SYNC_API_KEY` for data sync calls

Future production deployment should replace static env JWT usage with a proper
user-scoped or service-scoped auth flow.

+# AGENTS.md - Designflow MCP Operating Guide
+
+## Project summary
+
+Designflow MCP is a TypeScript/Node MCP server for AI clients that need Designflow company context, API documentation, and read-only lookups into the existing Designflow BFF/backend services. Future developers and AI sessions use it to discover a small set of always-on MCP tools, search a hidden operation registry, and query Designflow without loading a huge `tools/list` into every session.
+
+## Multi-model AI note
+
+There is no universal ignore-file standard across AI coding tools.
+
+`.claudeignore` works for Claude Code.
+
+When using any other AI tool, paste this file as your first message and follow the instructions in the "What to ignore" section.
+
+## Documentation map: what to read for each task
+
+Always start with:
+
+- `AGENTS.md`
+
+Then load additional docs only when relevant:
+
+| Task / question | Read these docs | Usually do not need |
+|---|---|---|
+| Quick repo orientation | `README.md`, `AGENTS.md` | Deep docs under `docs/` unless task requires them |
+| Modify app behavior or project-owned code | `AGENTS.md`, relevant folder-level `README.md`, `docs/architecture.md` if system design is affected | `docs/deployment.md` unless deploy behavior changes |
+| Add or change configuration, env vars, feature flags, secrets, or runtime settings | `AGENTS.md`, `docs/configuration.md`, `docs/deployment.md` if prod/runtime env is affected | Unrelated architecture docs |
+| Change local setup, dev scripts, test/lint/debug workflow, package scripts, or tooling | `AGENTS.md`, `docs/development.md`, relevant package/config files | `docs/deployment.md` unless CI/CD changes |
+| Change deployment, Docker, CI/CD, hosting, release flow, rollback, or runtime environment | `AGENTS.md`, `docs/deployment.md`, `docs/configuration.md`, relevant workflow/deployment files | Local-only development docs unless needed |
+| Change database schema, migrations, models, external IDs, or data flow | `AGENTS.md`, `docs/architecture.md`, `docs/configuration.md` if env/config is affected, relevant migration/model docs | Deployment docs unless rollout/deploy behavior changes |
+| Investigate bugs or incidents | `AGENTS.md`, relevant docs based on affected area, `HANDOFF.md` if present, Critical incidents section in `AGENTS.md` | Unrelated folder-level READMEs |
+| Continue unfinished work | `AGENTS.md`, `HANDOFF.md`, relevant docs named inside `HANDOFF.md` | Docs unrelated to the handoff scope |
+| Work in a subfolder with its own README | `AGENTS.md`, that folder-level `README.md`, and only broader docs referenced there | Other folder-level READMEs |
+| Claude Code session | `CLAUDE.md`, then `AGENTS.md` | Other docs unless task requires them |
+| Documentation-only cleanup | `AGENTS.md`, `README.md`, affected docs under `docs/`, folder-level READMEs only where relevant | Source files except as needed to verify accuracy |
+
+Rules:
+
+- Keep this map task-based.
+- Do not make future sessions read every Markdown file.
+- If `HANDOFF.md` exists, read it for continuation work.
+- Update this map when documentation files are added, removed, renamed, or repurposed.
+
+## Repository structure
+
+Project-owned code:
+
+- `src/index.ts` - process entry point, transport selection, Express HTTP server, stdio startup.
+- `src/server.ts` - MCP server factory and the always-on MCP tool registrations.
+- `src/clients/designflow-client.ts` - HTTP client for existing Designflow BFF/backend services.
+- `src/config/settings.ts` - environment and `tools-config.json` loading.
+- `src/registry/` - hidden capability registry, search, grouping, doc loading, and metadata.
+- `src/types.ts` - shared TypeScript types.
+- `tools-config.json` - hidden capability enablement.
+
+Generated code and build artifacts:
+
+- `dist/` - TypeScript build output from `npm run build`; do not edit.
+- `package-lock.json` - generated dependency lockfile; update through `npm install`.
+
+Third-party/vendor/framework code:
+
+- `node_modules/` - installed npm packages; never edit or load into AI context.
+
+Docs:
+
+- `README.md` - quick entry point and orientation.
+- `AGENTS.md` - canonical AI/developer operating guide and doc router.
+- `CLAUDE.md` - Claude Code-specific instructions only.
+- `docs/architecture.md` - system design, components, data flow, constraints.
+- `docs/development.md` - local setup, run/test/debug workflow.
+- `docs/configuration.md` - environment variables, config files, feature flags.
+- `docs/deployment.md` - real deployment status, Docker image build path, and unknowns.
+
+Scripts and tooling:
+
+- `package.json` - npm scripts and dependency inventory.
+- `tsconfig.json` - TypeScript compiler settings.
+
+Migrations:
+
+- None. This service has no database and no migration system.
+
+Deployment files:
+
+- `Dockerfile` - production image build for the MCP service.
+- `.dockerignore` - Docker build-context exclusions.
+
+## Prime Directive: custom-code boundary
+
+Our custom code lives here:
+
+- `src/index.ts`
+- `src/server.ts`
+- `src/clients/`
+- `src/config/`
+- `src/registry/`
+- `src/types.ts`
+- `docs/`
+- `README.md`
+- `AGENTS.md`
+- `CLAUDE.md`
+- `tools-config.json`
+- `package.json`
+- `tsconfig.json`
+- `Dockerfile`
+- `.dockerignore`
+- `.gitignore`
+- `.claudeignore`
+- `.cursorignore`
+
+Everything else requires justification before touching.
+
+Do not put Designflow business logic into third-party packages, generated build output, or random new framework directories. The MCP service should remain a thin lazy-discovery layer over existing Designflow APIs and docs.
+
+## Core modification inventory
+
+| File | Change made | Why it was necessary | Risk during upgrades |
+|---|---|---|---|
+| n/a | No files outside project-owned areas have been modified. | n/a | n/a |
+
+## Task-to-file navigation: what to edit for common changes
+
+| Task | Files to touch | Files not to touch |
+|---|---|---|
+| Add a read-only hidden MCP capability | `src/registry/tool-defs.ts`, `src/registry/groups.ts` if a new group/keyword is needed, `tools-config.json`, tests if behavior is added | `src/server.ts` unless adding an always-on tool is explicitly justified |
+| Add an always-on MCP tool | `src/server.ts`, `AGENTS.md`, `README.md`, tests | `src/registry/tool-defs.ts` alone; hidden operations must not become always-on accidentally |
+| Change capability search behavior | `src/registry/catalog.ts`, `src/registry/groups.ts`, tests | API client files unless service calls changed |
+| Change Designflow API base URLs or auth headers | `src/clients/designflow-client.ts`, `src/config/settings.ts`, `.env.example`, `docs/configuration.md` | Hidden tool definitions unless endpoint contracts changed |
+| Add a documentation-backed resource/tool | `src/registry/doc-reader.ts`, `src/registry/tool-defs.ts`, `tools-config.json`, `docs/architecture.md` if data flow changes | `node_modules/`, `dist/` |
+| Change local run/build workflow | `package.json`, `docs/development.md`, `README.md` | `docs/deployment.md` unless deployment behavior changes |
+| Change Docker image build/runtime | `Dockerfile`, `.dockerignore`, `docs/deployment.md`, `docs/configuration.md` if env vars change | Local-only dev docs unless scripts change |
+| Add write/destructive operations | `src/registry/tool-defs.ts`, `src/server.ts` only if confirmation enforcement changes, `AGENTS.md`, `docs/architecture.md`, tests | Do not enable writes in `tools-config.json` without preview/confirmation/audit behavior |
+| Add or change ignore rules | `.gitignore`, `.dockerignore` where relevant, `.claudeignore`, `.cursorignore`, `AGENTS.md` What to ignore | Source files unless generated/build paths changed |
+
+## Data model and external identifiers
+
+| Entity/System | Identifier | Where defined | Notes |
+|---|---|---|---|
+| MCP server name | `designflow-mcp` | `src/server.ts`, `package.json` | Name clients see during MCP initialization. |
+| MCP version | `0.1.0` | `package.json`, `src/server.ts` | Keep in sync when publishing versioned releases. |
+| HTTP MCP endpoint | `POST /mcp` | `src/index.ts` | Stateless Streamable HTTP endpoint. |
+| HTTP health endpoint | `GET /health` | `src/index.ts` | Returns service status JSON. |
+| Default HTTP port | `3010` | `src/index.ts`, `.env.example`, `docs/configuration.md` | Override with `MCP_PORT`. |
+| Core API base | `DESIGNFLOW_CORE_BASE_URL` | `.env.example`, `src/config/settings.ts` | Defaults to `http://localhost:5004/api/core`. |
+| Item Master API base | `DESIGNFLOW_ITEM_MASTER_BASE_URL` | `.env.example`, `src/config/settings.ts` | Defaults to `http://localhost:5004/api/item_master`. |
+| Tracking API base | `DESIGNFLOW_TRACKING_BASE_URL` | `.env.example`, `src/config/settings.ts` | Defaults to `http://localhost:5004/api/tracking`. |
+| Data Sync API base | `DESIGNFLOW_DATA_SYNC_BASE_URL` | `.env.example`, `src/config/settings.ts` | Defaults to `http://localhost:5004/api/data_sync`. |
+| User JWT env var | `DESIGNFLOW_USER_JWT` | `.env.example`, `src/clients/designflow-client.ts` | Optional for docs-only tools; required for user-scoped API tools. Secret value must not be committed. |
+| Data Sync API key env var | `DFLOW_SYNC_API_KEY` | `.env.example`, `src/clients/designflow-client.ts` | Required only for data-sync backed tools. Secret value must not be committed. |
+| Hidden capability config | `tools-config.json` | repo root, `src/config/settings.ts` | Controls enabled hidden tools without code changes. |
+| Always-on tools | `health`, `list_capabilities`, `get_capability_details`, `tool_search`, `invoke_tool` | `src/server.ts` | Keep this list intentionally small. |
+
+## Container and service inventory
+
+| Container/service | Purpose | Managed by | App/project ID | Image/source |
+|---|---|---|---|---|
+| `designflow-mcp` Docker image | Runs the MCP HTTP server with `node dist/index.js --http` | `Dockerfile`; no deployed runtime found in this repo | Unknown; no Cloud Run service or GitHub Actions workflow is present in this repo | Built from `node:22-slim` and this repo source |
+
+No database, queue, worker, or sidecar services are defined in this repo.
+
+## What to ignore
+
+Do not spend AI context on:
+
+- `node_modules/`
+- `dist/`
+- `.cache/`
+- `coverage/`
+- `.env`
+- `.env.*` except `.env.example`
+- npm debug logs
+
+These entries must stay aligned across `.gitignore`, `.claudeignore`, `.cursorignore`, and the relevant Docker exclusions in `.dockerignore`.
+
+## Intentional quirks and non-obvious decisions
+
+### Lazy capability registry
+
+Looks like:
+The server only exposes five MCP tools even though `tools-config.json` enables many Designflow operations.
+
+Actually:
+Only `health`, `list_capabilities`, `get_capability_details`, `tool_search`, and `invoke_tool` are registered with MCP. Real operations live in `src/registry/tool-defs.ts` and are executed by `invoke_tool`.
+
+Why:
+Large eager `tools/list` payloads waste session context and can degrade AI behavior. Returning operation contracts as text through discovery tools keeps the initial MCP surface small.
+
+Do not change because:
+Registering every Designflow operation directly would recreate the context-bloat problem this service is designed to avoid.
+
+### No dynamic tool registration
+
+Looks like:
+The service ignores MCP `tools/list_changed` as a way to register matching tools after search.
+
+Actually:
+Discovery tools return structured contracts as text, and `invoke_tool` executes by exact operation name.
+
+Why:
+Real clients may cache the initial `tools/list` and not reliably refetch after notifications.
+
+Do not change because:
+Dynamic registration can add complexity without delivering durable context savings.
+
+### Stateless HTTP transport
+
+Looks like:
+`GET /mcp` and `DELETE /mcp` return `405`, and each `POST /mcp` creates a new server/transport.
+
+Actually:
+The HTTP mode is a stateless Streamable HTTP implementation using `sessionIdGenerator: undefined`.
+
+Why:
+The current tool set is read-only and does not require server-initiated notifications or resumable sessions. Stateless mode is simpler for a future Cloud Run deployment.
+
+Do not change because:
+Adding session state increases operational complexity. If stateful features become necessary, preserve the lazy registry and document the reason.
+
+### Documentation tools read sibling repos
+
+Looks like:
+`src/registry/doc-reader.ts` reaches outside this repo to `../designflow-*` paths.
+
+Actually:
+Doc-backed hidden tools read the API reference files in sibling Designflow repos checked out under `D:\repos\dflow`.
+
+Why:
+The MCP server is intended to help AI sessions understand the whole Designflow system, not only this service.
+
+Do not change because:
+Replacing this with copied docs would create stale duplicates. If the workspace layout changes, update `DOC_PATHS` and document the new layout.
+
+## Credentials and environment
+
+| Variable | Purpose | Stored where | Required in dev | Required in prod |
+|---|---|---|---|---|
+| `MCP_PORT` | HTTP server port for `--http` mode | `.env`, Cloud Run/service env if deployed | no; defaults to `3010` | yes if deployed port differs from `3010` |
+| `DESIGNFLOW_CORE_BASE_URL` | Base URL for core Designflow API calls | `.env`, runtime env | no; defaults to local BFF core URL | yes when deployed |
+| `DESIGNFLOW_ITEM_MASTER_BASE_URL` | Base URL for item-master API calls | `.env`, runtime env | no; defaults to local BFF item-master URL | yes when deployed |
+| `DESIGNFLOW_TRACKING_BASE_URL` | Base URL for tracking API calls | `.env`, runtime env | no; defaults to local BFF tracking URL | yes when deployed |
+| `DESIGNFLOW_DATA_SYNC_BASE_URL` | Base URL for data-sync API calls | `.env`, runtime env | no; defaults to local BFF data-sync URL | yes when deployed |
+| `DESIGNFLOW_USER_JWT` | Optional user JWT for user-scoped Designflow API tools | `.env`, secret/runtime env | only for API-backed tools | yes for user-scoped deployment; storage mechanism unknown |
+| `DFLOW_SYNC_API_KEY` | Optional data-sync API key | `.env`, secret/runtime env | only for data-sync tools | yes if data-sync tools are enabled in production |
+| `DESIGNFLOW_MCP_ENABLED_TOOLS` | Comma-separated extra hidden tools to enable | `.env`, runtime env | no | no |
+| `DESIGNFLOW_MCP_DISABLED_TOOLS` | Comma-separated hidden tools to disable | `.env`, runtime env | no | no |
+| `NODE_ENV` | Runtime mode in Docker image | `Dockerfile`, runtime env | no | set to `production` by Dockerfile |
+
+Do not commit secret values. `.env.example` must contain names and safe defaults only.
+
+## Deployment
+
+Current real deployment status:
+
+- No GitHub Actions workflow exists in this repo.
+- No `cloudbuild.yaml` exists in this repo.
+- No Cloud Run service name, GCP project ID, or deployed URL is defined in this repo.
+- `Dockerfile` can build a production image that runs `node dist/index.js --http`.
+- Runtime environment variables would need to be supplied by the future deployment platform.
+- SSH deployment is not documented and is not a normal deployment path for this repo.
+
+Known image build path:
+
+```bash
+docker build -t designflow-mcp .
+docker run --rm -p 3010:3010 --env-file .env designflow-mcp
+```
+
+Rollback method:
+
+- Unknown because no deployment platform is configured in this repo.
+- Verify by checking for future `.github/workflows/`, `cloudbuild.yaml`, infrastructure code, or the deployment platform dashboard.
+
+Deploy trigger:
+
+- Unknown/not configured in this repo.
+
+## Critical incidents
+
+No production incidents are known for this repo. This service has not been documented as deployed.
+
+Potential risk already addressed:
+
+### 2026-06-08 Tools-list context bloat avoided
+
+What happened:
+This service was scaffolded after another MCP server showed that eager registration of many tool schemas can consume substantial session context.
+
+Impact:
+No Designflow production impact. The lesson shaped this initial architecture.
+
+Root cause:
+Large MCP `tools/list` surfaces are expensive for AI sessions.
+
+Recovery:
+Designflow MCP was built with a lazy registry and five always-on tools.
+
+Rule added to prevent recurrence:
+Do not eagerly register hidden Designflow operations. Use discovery tools plus `invoke_tool`.
+
+## Pending work
+
+| Status | Item | Owner/next action |
+|---|---|---|
+| open | Create/attach GitHub repository and remote | Create `popcre/designflow-mcp` or confirm target repo, then push `sandbox-albert`. |
+| open | Configure production deployment | Add the real deployment workflow/platform details after the service is deployed. |
+| open | Replace static `DESIGNFLOW_USER_JWT` production auth | Decide user-scoped vs service-scoped auth for remote MCP clients before production. |
+| open | Add unit tests for registry/search/client behavior | Add test runner and focused tests before expanding functionality. |
+
*** End Patch
